claim: Obsidian CEO Steph Ango (kepano) avoids folders and organizes via a categories property plus reusable property names (created, author, genre, type, topic) so values stay queryable across categories.
quote: My notes are primarily organized using the `categories` property... I avoid folders because many of my entries belong to more than one area of thought. Property names and values should aim to be reusable across categories.

claim: Long-term Johnny.Decimal users in Obsidian typically combine folder/index hierarchy for primary structure with hierarchical tags reserved for cross-cutting themes that span multiple JD codes.
quote: use tags for similar items that technically belong in different IDs ... organization of objects that don't logically belong in the same part of your J.D system.

claim: Johnny.Decimal caps at exactly 100 permanent addresses (10 areas x 10 categories) and is sold as scaling by permanence rather than infinite expansion, which is the structural ceiling personal-vault adopters hit.
quote: designed to scale. With 100 permanent addresses, you have room to grow.

claim: Practitioner consensus for at-scale vaults: folders only for high-level separation (max two levels), MOCs for navigation, tags as signals/filters rather than primary structure.
quote: Tags ... function as signals, not structure ... useful for discovery and filtering ... but shouldn't be your primary organization system.

claim: A common pattern from large-vault users is to reserve hashtags for note-status indicators (inbox, seedling, evergreen) and use MOCs/links for topical structure.
quote: hashtags solely for indicators of status ... inputs needing to be processed ... seedlings ... evergreen notes.

claim: Nick Milo's LYT framework popularized the rule of thumb to spawn an MOC once 5+ notes cluster on a topic, framing MOCs as the antidote to flat-vault overload.
quote: Links keep notes from getting trapped in a single folder; MOCs keep you from getting lost in the web of links.

claim: The dominant PARA failure mode in Obsidian is Projects and Resources collapsing into each other — Resources becomes a graveyard of interesting-but-not-actionable material while Projects stays artificially clean.
quote: Projects and Resources collapse into each other, with everything interesting becoming a Resource while the Projects folder stays artificially clean.

claim: Hierarchical tag prefixes like #project/active, #project/soon are the de-facto extraction-friendly convention because Dataview can FROM a parent tag and pull all children.
quote: pull all projects using the #project tag, or scope it to a particular status such as #project/soon or #project/active.

claim: Tag-hygiene tooling has emerged specifically because casing/separator inconsistency (snake vs kebab, mixed case) is a known scale problem; bulk tag managers now enforce taxonomy rules across the vault.
quote: bulk renaming, enforcing casing rules (lowercase/uppercase), swapping separators (snake/kebab), and generating master tag lists.

claim: Empirically, vaults with hundreds of tags (one user reported ~400) report no slowdown, so taxonomy bloat is a cognitive/maintenance cost rather than a performance ceiling.
quote: one user with about 400 tags in their vault didn't see any slowdown.

claim: Steph Ango's vault uses 50+ category-specific note templates (Game Studio, Restaurant, Job Interview) anchored by Properties and Bases rather than folder taxonomy.
quote: over 50 templates for notes, for every category of thing, from 'Game Studio' to 'Restaurant' to 'Job Interview'.
